claims.claim_text: The opening Ceph outage anecdote presents a named scenario with precise cluster size, resource usage, network impact, and downtime as if it were a documented real event.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: A fintech startup ran six microservices on three bare-metal servers. They deployed Rook-Ceph because "everyone uses Ceph." Within a week, the Ceph monitors consumed 2 GB of RAM each, OSD recovery traffic saturated their 10 GbE links during a node reboot, and a junior engineer accidentally deleted the mon keyring while debugging. Total downtime was 14 hours, two of which were spent finding a backup of the keyring on a laptop.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A small bare-metal team can be a poor fit for a full distributed storage stack if it adopts Ceph before it actually needs Ceph's failure-domain and shared-storage features.
claims.rationale: This is a fake-precise operational anecdote with no attributable primary source.

claims.claim_text: The follow-up Longhorn success story presents precise deployment time, replica layout, pod-count reduction, and incident-free duration as if it were a documented real event.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2
claims.anchor_text: They replaced Ceph with Longhorn the following weekend. Deployment took 20 minutes. Each microservice received a three-replica volume backed by local NVMe. The total storage overhead dropped from nine pods (three MONs, three MGRs, three OSDs, plus the operator) down to a single per-node DaemonSet. Six months later, they have had zero storage incidents, and the on-call rotation no longer dreads pages from the storage layer.
claims.suggested_rewrite: After switching from a heavier distributed stack to a simpler replicated local-storage system, a small team can reduce storage-specific operational overhead substantially.
claims.rationale: This is another unattributed war story with false precision and named implementation details.

claims.claim_text: The module claims that this exact pattern repeats every month across small clusters.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 3
claims.anchor_text: That story repeats across small clusters every month, and it points at a deeper truth: not every on-premises cluster needs a distributed storage system.
claims.suggested_rewrite: That pattern shows up repeatedly in smaller clusters, and it points at a deeper truth: not every on-premises cluster needs a distributed storage system.
claims.rationale: The monthly frequency is an uncited quantitative generalization.

claims.claim_text: local-path-provisioner dynamically provisions node-local storage using helper scripts/pods and relies on PV node affinity to keep workloads on the provisioned node.
claims.rationale: The upstream repository documents helper pod setup/teardown behavior and node-affinity-based local PV placement.

claims.claim_text: local-path-provisioner does not enforce requested PVC capacity limits.
claims.rationale: The upstream README explicitly states that capacity limits are ignored.

claims.claim_text: TopoLVM provides dynamic LVM-backed local volumes with topology-aware scheduling, extended scheduling, expansion, and snapshot support.
claims.rationale: The TopoLVM upstream README lists topology, extended scheduler, expansion, and snapshot support.

claims.claim_text: OpenEBS LocalPV-LVM is an LVM-backed local CSI engine with LVMVolume CRs, snapshot/thin-provisioning support, and local node-bound semantics.
claims.span_hint: section: OpenEBS Local Engines / Installing LocalPV-LVM
claims.rationale: The upstream repository describes the LVM-backed architecture, LVMVolume CRs, and node-local behavior.

claims.claim_text: The module states snapshot restore as unconditional LocalPV-LVM behavior, but current support is version- and volume-type-dependent.
claims.span_hint: section: OpenEBS Local Engines / Worked Example: Provision, Verify, Snapshot, Restore
claims.anchor_text: Now restore. With LocalPV-LVM you restore by creating a new PVC whose `dataSource` references the snapshot, which produces a fresh LV on the same node populated from the snapshot's CoW state.
claims.suggested_rewrite: If you are running an OpenEBS LocalPV-LVM release that supports snapshot restore for thin volumes, you restore by creating a new PVC whose `dataSource` references the snapshot.
claims.rationale: The capability exists in newer releases, but the text overstates it by omitting version and thin-volume constraints.

claims.claim_text: The module prescribes an alert threshold of 80% thin-pool utilization as if it were a sourced fact.
claims.span_hint: section: OpenEBS Local Engines / Worked Example, failure modes paragraph
claims.anchor_text: you must monitor `Data%` on the thin pool with `lvs -o name,data_percent,metadata_percent` and alert before it hits 80%.
claims.suggested_rewrite: you must monitor `Data%` on the thin pool with `lvs -o name,data_percent,metadata_percent` and alert well before the pool is near full.
claims.rationale: The exact 80% cutoff is an unsourced numeric threshold.

claims.claim_text: The LocalPV-ZFS section makes overly specific claims about compression codecs and self-healing mirrors.
claims.span_hint: section: OpenEBS Local Engines / LocalPV-ZFS in One Pass
claims.anchor_text: native compression (lz4/zstd), and self-healing mirrors when you run multi-disk pools.
claims.suggested_rewrite: native compression and copy-on-write snapshots, plus the broader ZFS integrity model when you build pools appropriately.
claims.rationale: The specific algorithm list and mirror behavior are too precise for the source quality shown in the module.

claims.claim_text: Longhorn creates a dedicated controller per volume and synchronously replicates data across replicas on multiple nodes.
claims.span_hint: section: Longhorn: Replicated Storage Without Ceph / Architecture
claims.rationale: The upstream Longhorn README describes dedicated storage controllers per volume and synchronous replication across nodes.

claims.claim_text: Longhorn requires iSCSI initiator packages such as open-iscsi or iscsi-initiator-utils on cluster nodes.
claims.span_hint: section: Longhorn: Replicated Storage Without Ceph / Longhorn Deployment
claims.rationale: This is a real installation prerequisite, but the primary documentation is on longhorn.io, which is outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Longhorn RWX volumes are exposed through an NFS share-manager layer rather than a native shared block filesystem.
claims.rationale: The upstream repository identifies Longhorn Share Manager as the NFS component that exposes volumes as RWX.

claims.claim_text: Backup retention for Longhorn backupstores should be managed by Longhorn itself rather than external bucket lifecycle policies.
claims.span_hint: section: Longhorn: Replicated Storage Without Ceph / Automated Backups and Restore
claims.rationale: The official guidance exists, but it lives on longhorn.io, which is outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Longhorn does not restore recurring jobs from backups by default unless restore-volume-recurring-jobs is enabled or schedules are re-applied.
claims.span_hint: section: Longhorn: Replicated Storage Without Ceph / Automated Backups and Restore
claims.rationale: The official behavior is documented, but only on an off-allowlist host.

claims.claim_text: The comparison table gives exact per-node RAM overhead figures for five different storage systems.
claims.anchor_text: | Overhead (RAM per node) | ~10 MB | ~50 MB | ~50 MB | ~100 MB | ~500 MB |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Overhead (RAM per node) | Very low | Low | Low | Moderate | Higher |
claims.rationale: These exact overhead numbers are workload- and version-sensitive and are not backed by a cited benchmark.

claims.claim_text: Topology-constrained local storage should use WaitForFirstConsumer because Immediate can bind before the scheduler knows where the consuming Pod should run.
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes storage-class documentation explicitly explains WaitForFirstConsumer for topology-constrained backends such as local storage.

claims.claim_text: The decision tree gives precise failover timing claims for Longhorn and Ceph RBD.
claims.span_hint: section: When to Use Local vs Distributed Storage
claims.anchor_text: The last branch is a cost-versus-RTO trade-off: Longhorn fails over in tens of seconds, Ceph RBD with proper tuning can fail over in single-digit seconds, and that gap is sometimes the deciding factor.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The last branch is a cost-versus-RTO trade-off: replicated local storage and pooled distributed storage can deliver different failover characteristics, and that gap may matter for latency-sensitive workloads.
claims.rationale: These exact timing numbers need controlled benchmarks and environment details, not generic prose.

claims.claim_text: K3s ships Rancher Local Path Provisioner as an out-of-box local storage option.
claims.rationale: The claim is real, but the primary K3s documentation host is outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: The module claims that over 100,000 production edge clusters rely on local-path-provisioner today.
claims.anchor_text: - **local-path-provisioner powers all default k3s installations.** When you run k3s on an edge device, the `local-path` StorageClass is created automatically, and over 100,000 edge clusters in production today rely on it for lightweight persistent storage without any distributed-storage overhead. K3s ships it as the out-of-box answer because the typical k3s cluster is a single node and there is nothing useful to replicate to.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **local-path-provisioner ships with K3s as the default lightweight local-storage option.** In small or edge-oriented K3s deployments, it provides an out-of-the-box StorageClass without requiring a distributed storage layer.
claims.rationale: The production-cluster count is a precise adoption statistic with no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The TopoLVM origin story attributes the project to Cybozu and adds uncited claims about related projects and fleet scale.
claims.anchor_text: - **TopoLVM was created by Cybozu**, the Japanese enterprise software company that also produced the `coil` CNI plugin and the `accurate` namespace controller. Cybozu runs hundreds of Kubernetes clusters on bare metal and needed a storage solution that enforced LVM quotas without the complexity of a distributed system; the design choice to integrate with the scheduler via webhook rather than via a CSI extension is a direct consequence of that operational pragmatism.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **TopoLVM was built for bare-metal Kubernetes environments that need LVM-backed local volumes with scheduler awareness.** Its design centers on pairing local LVM capacity with topology-aware placement rather than on building a distributed storage pool.
claims.rationale: This is an uncited origin story with named-entity and scale claims.

claims.claim_text: Longhorn joined CNCF as a sandbox project in October 2019 and moved to incubating on November 4, 2021.
claims.rationale: The CNCF project page gives the acceptance and incubation dates directly.

claims.claim_text: SUSE acquired Rancher in 2020.
claims.rationale: SUSE's acquisition announcement is the primary vendor source for this fact.

claims.claim_text: The module claims that Longhorn's architecture is what lets two-engineer platform teams run it at production scale.
claims.anchor_text: that per-volume blast radius is the architectural property that lets two-engineer platform teams operate Longhorn at production scale.
claims.suggested_rewrite: that per-volume blast radius is one reason smaller platform teams often find Longhorn easier to operate than pooled distributed storage.
claims.rationale: This is an unsupported staffing-scale claim disguised as a general fact.

claims.claim_text: The OpenEBS history bullet asserts a contested CNCF milestone and mixes it with a broader architectural claim.
claims.anchor_text: - **OpenEBS was the first CNCF sandbox storage project** (joined in 2019) and pioneered the concept of "Container Attached Storage": the idea that storage controllers should run as containers alongside application containers rather than as a separate infrastructure layer. That choice is why OpenEBS's local engines compose so cleanly with Helm, GitOps, and per-namespace RBAC, where traditional storage appliances assume a separate operations team and a separate lifecycle.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **OpenEBS helped popularize the idea of container-attached storage.** Its design centers on running storage controllers as Kubernetes-managed components rather than as a separate storage appliance layer.
claims.rationale: The CNCF history is not stable as written, and the bullet overclaims beyond the clearly attributable architectural concept.

claims.claim_text: The Common Mistakes table recommends 10 GbE minimum for Longhorn as if it were a hard vendor requirement.
claims.anchor_text: | Longhorn on slow networks | Synchronous replication adds write latency proportional to network RTT | Ensure dedicated storage network or 10 GbE minimum |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Longhorn on slow networks | Synchronous replication adds write latency proportional to network RTT | Ensure low-latency networking and validate performance under expected load |
claims.rationale: The exact 10 GbE floor is an uncited performance prescription.

claims.claim_text: The Common Mistakes table gives an exact Ceph pod-count threshold and a hard cluster-size cutoff for choosing alternatives.
claims.anchor_text: | Choosing Ceph for 3-node clusters | Ceph overhead dominates small clusters (10+ pods just for storage) | Use Longhorn or TopoLVM for clusters under six nodes |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Choosing Ceph for very small clusters | Ceph overhead can dominate small clusters | Re-evaluate whether Longhorn, TopoLVM, or another simpler backend fits better |
claims.rationale: Both the pod count and the node-count cutoff are uncited benchmark-style claims.

claims.claim_text: If Longhorn cannot schedule all requested replicas, the volume remains under-replicated or degraded until placement succeeds.
claims.rationale: The official behavior is documented by Longhorn, but only on an off-allowlist documentation host.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Storage Classes
further_reading.why_relevant: Canonical reference for volumeBindingMode, topology-aware binding, and StorageClass semantics.

further_reading.title: local-path-provisioner Upstream README
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for local-path behavior, helper-pod lifecycle, and the lack of capacity enforcement.

further_reading.title: TopoLVM Upstream README
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for TopoLVM capabilities such as topology-aware scheduling, expansion, and snapshots.

further_reading.title: Longhorn Upstream README
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary source for Longhorn's per-volume controller model, synchronous replication, and RWX share-manager architecture.

notes: Several accurate product-specific claims in this module currently require allowlist expansion because their official docs live on longhorn.io, openebs.io, or docs.k3s.io rather than on an allowlisted host.
